docs.title: shark-cli
docs.text: A professional, modular CLI for the Sharktech Cloud Provider API.

docs.title: What is shark-cli?
docs.text: shark-cli lets you manage your entire Sharktech Cloud infrastructure from the terminal — virtual machines, networks, load balancers, secrets, Kubernetes clusters, metrics, and more.

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:
• 14 command groups covering all major OpenStack services
  • 100+ sub-commands for full lifecycle management
  • Rich terminal output — coloured tables powered by Rich
  • Shell auto-completion — Bash, Zsh, and Fish
  • Secure configuration — env vars take precedence; config file written with 0600 permissions

docs.title: Supported Services
docs.text: Service Command Backend Compute shark server Nova Flavors shark flavor Nova Images shark image Glance Networks shark network Neutron Key Pairs shark keypair Nova Volumes shark volume Cinder Security Groups shark security-group Neutron Floating IPs shark floating-ip Neutron Load Balancers shark loadbalancer Octavia Secrets shark secret Barbican Clusters shark cluster Magnum Metrics shark metric Gnocchi Service Catalog shark catalog Keystone

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
pip install .
shark setup
shark server list

See the Getting Started guide for full installation and configuration instructions.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.9+
  • A Sharktech Cloud account with API credentials

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pipPoetry 
pip install .

poetry install

After installation the shark command is available globally.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
You need credentials from your Sharktech Client Area (cloud service information page):

Field Description Example Auth URL Keystone endpoint https://cloud-xx.sharktech.net:5000 Username OpenStack user myuser Password OpenStack password — Domain ID OpenStack domain mydomain Project ID OpenStack project myproject

docs.title: Interactive setup
docs.text:
shark setup

This prompts for all fields and stores them in ~/.shark/config.yaml (permissions 600).

docs.title: Environment variables
docs.text:
export SHARK_AUTH_URL="https://cloud-xx.sharktech.net:5000"
export SHARK_USERNAME="myuser"
export SHARK_PASSWORD="mypassword"
export SHARK_DOMAIN_ID="mydomain"
export SHARK_PROJECT_ID="myproject"

Tip

Environment variables always take precedence over the config file.

docs.title: Shell Auto-Completion
docs.text:
BashZshFish

Add to ~/.bashrc:

eval "$(_SHARK_COMPLETE=bash_source shark)"

Add to ~/.zshrc:

eval "$(_SHARK_COMPLETE=zsh_source shark)"

_SHARK_COMPLETE=fish_source shark > ~/.config/fish/completions/shark.fish

You can also run shark completion <shell> to display these instructions.

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
shark --version
shark catalog        # List available service endpoints
shark server list    # List your compute instances

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Browse the command reference for detailed usage of each service.

docs.title: Command Reference
docs.text:
shark-cli organises commands by OpenStack service. Every command supports --help for inline documentation.

shark --help              # List all command groups
shark server --help       # List all server sub-commands
shark server create --help  # Detailed help for a specific command

docs.title: Command Groups
docs.text: Group Commands Description server 28 Compute instances — full lifecycle flavor 1 List available flavors image 11 Glance image management network 21 Networks, subnets, ports & routers keypair 5 SSH key pair management volume 13 Block storage volumes & snapshots security-group 7 Security groups & rules floating-ip 6 Floating IP allocation & association loadbalancer 18 Load balancers, listeners, pools, members & health monitors secret 8 Secrets & containers (Barbican) cluster 10 Kubernetes clusters & templates (Magnum) metric 8 Metrics, measures & resources (Gnocchi) catalog 1 Service endpoint discovery

docs.title: Confirmation prompts
docs.text:
Destructive commands (delete, remove) ask for confirmation. Skip with -y:

shark server delete <id> -y
shark loadbalancer delete <id> --cascade -y

docs.title: Output format
docs.text:
All commands output rich, coloured tables. Pipe to less -R for paging:

shark server list | less -R

docs.title: Service Catalog — shark catalog
docs.text:
List available service endpoints from the Keystone identity catalog. The catalog is returned by Keystone during authentication and lists every OpenStack service available in your project, along with its type, interface, and endpoint URL.

This command is useful for:

  • Debugging — verifying which services are available and their URLs
  • Discovery — finding endpoint URLs for services not yet covered by shark
  • Troubleshooting — confirming connectivity to specific services

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
shark catalog

Output columns: Service (name), Type (e.g. compute, network, identity), Interface (public, internal, admin), URL.

docs.title: Example output
docs.text:
┌──────────┬───────────┬───────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Service  │ Type      │ Interface │ URL                                   │
├──────────┼───────────┼───────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
│ nova     │ compute   │ public    │ https://compute.example.com/v2.1      │
│ neutron  │ network   │ public    │ https://network.example.com           │
│ glance   │ image     │ public    │ https://image.example.com             │
│ cinder   │ volumev3  │ public    │ https://volume.example.com/v3         │
│ keystone │ identity  │ public    │ https://identity.example.com/v3       │
│ octavia  │ load-bal… │ public    │ https://lb.example.com                │
│ barbican │ key-mana… │ public    │ https://secrets.example.com           │
│ magnum   │ containe… │ public    │ https://container.example.com         │
│ gnocchi  │ metric    │ public    │ https://metric.example.com            │
└──────────┴───────────┴───────────┴───────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Clusters — shark cluster
docs.text: Manage Kubernetes clusters and cluster templates (Magnum). Magnum provisions production-ready Kubernetes clusters on top of the Sharktech Cloud infrastructure, including master/worker nodes, networking, and load balancing.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all Kubernetes clusters with their status, node/master count, and template.

shark cluster list

docs.title: show
docs.text:
Display detailed cluster properties: UUID, status, status reason, COE version, API address, node/master addresses, keypair, stack ID, and timestamps.

shark cluster show <cluster-id>

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Create a new Kubernetes cluster from a cluster template. The cluster creation is asynchronous — use show to track progress.

shark cluster create my-k8s --template <template-id>

shark cluster create my-k8s \
  --template <template-id> \
  --node-count 3 \
  --master-count 1 \
  --keypair my-keypair

shark cluster create prod \
  --template <id> \
  --master-count 3 \
  --node-count 5 \
  --flavor <flavor-id> \
  --master-flavor <master-flavor-id>
Option Required Default Description --template yes — Cluster template UUID or name --node-count no 1 Number of worker nodes --master-count no 1 Number of master nodes --keypair no — SSH keypair name --timeout no 60 Creation timeout in minutes --flavor no — Node flavor (overrides template) --master-flavor no — Master flavor (overrides template)

docs.title: delete
docs.text:
Delete a cluster and all its resources (VMs, networks, volumes). The deletion is asynchronous.

shark cluster delete <cluster-id>
shark cluster delete <cluster-id> -y

docs.title: resize
docs.text:
Scale the number of worker nodes up or down. Masters are not affected.

shark cluster resize <cluster-id> --node-count 5
shark cluster resize <cluster-id> --node-count 1
Option Required Description --node-count yes New number of worker nodes

docs.title: kubeconfig
docs.text:
Display the cluster's API address and connection information. Use this to configure kubectl.

shark cluster kubeconfig <cluster-id>

Tip

The API address is only available once the cluster reaches CREATE_COMPLETE status.

docs.title: Cluster Templates
docs.text: Templates define the blueprint for clusters: base image, flavors, networking, COE, and driver options.

docs.title: template-list
docs.text:
List all cluster templates with their COE, image, network driver, and public flag.

shark cluster template-list

docs.title: template-show
docs.text:
Display detailed template properties: image, flavors, network config, DNS, labels, TLS settings.

shark cluster template-show <template-id>

docs.title: template-create
docs.text:
Create a cluster template. This defines the infrastructure blueprint that clusters will use.

# Basic template
shark cluster template-create k8s-template \
  --image <image-id> \
  --external-network <ext-net-id>

# Full-featured template
shark cluster template-create k8s-prod \
  --image <image-id> \
  --external-network <ext-net-id> \
  --coe kubernetes \
  --keypair my-keypair \
  --flavor <flavor-id> \
  --master-flavor <master-flavor-id> \
  --network-driver flannel \
  --docker-volume-size 50 \
  --dns 8.8.8.8 \
  --master-lb \
  --label boot_volume_size=20 \
  --label boot_volume_type=ceph-ssd
Option Required Default Description --image yes — Base image UUID or name --external-network yes — External network ID --coe no kubernetes kubernetes, swarm, mesos --keypair no — SSH keypair name --flavor no — Node flavor --master-flavor no — Master flavor --network-driver no — Network driver (flannel, calico, etc.) --docker-volume-size no — Docker volume size in GB --dns no 8.8.8.8 DNS nameserver --master-lb/--no-master-lb no True Enable master load balancing --floating-ip/--no-floating-ip no True Assign floating IPs --label no — Key=value label (repeatable)

Labels

Use --label key=value (repeatable) to set Magnum driver labels. Common labels:

  • boot_volume_size — Root volume size in GB (required for boot-from-volume clouds)
  • boot_volume_type — Volume type (e.g. ceph-ssd)
  • kube_tag — Kubernetes version tag

docs.title: template-delete
docs.text:
Delete a cluster template. It must not be in use by any cluster.

shark cluster template-delete <template-id>
shark cluster template-delete <template-id> -y

docs.title: Full Example: Deploy a Kubernetes Cluster
docs.text:
# 1. Create a template
shark cluster template-create k8s-template \
  --image <image-id> \
  --external-network <ext-net-id> \
  --keypair my-key \
  --flavor <flavor-id> \
  --label boot_volume_size=20

# 2. Create a cluster
shark cluster create my-k8s \
  --template k8s-template \
  --node-count 3 \
  --master-count 1

# 3. Monitor progress
shark cluster show my-k8s

# 4. Get connection info
shark cluster kubeconfig my-k8s

# 5. Scale up workers
shark cluster resize my-k8s --node-count 5

# 6. Tear down
shark cluster delete my-k8s -y

docs.title: Flavors — shark flavor
docs.text: List available compute flavors (Nova). Flavors define the hardware profile for a server: number of vCPUs, RAM, and root disk size. Use this command to find the right flavor ID before creating a server.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all available flavors, sorted by vCPUs and RAM. Output columns: ID, Name, vCPUs, RAM (MB), Disk (GB).

shark flavor list

Tip

A disk size of 0 means the flavor has no local disk — the server must boot from a Cinder volume (which shark server create does by default).

docs.title: Floating IPs — shark floating-ip
docs.text: Manage floating IPs (Neutron). Floating IPs are public IPv4 addresses that can be dynamically associated with servers to make them reachable from the internet. They are allocated from an external network and can be moved between servers at any time.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all floating IPs in the project with their public address, associated fixed IP, port, and status.

shark floating-ip list

docs.title: show
docs.text:
Display detailed properties of a floating IP: addresses, network, port, router, status, and timestamps.

shark floating-ip show <floating-ip-id>

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Allocate a new floating IP from an external network. The IP is reserved but not yet associated with any server.

shark floating-ip create --network <ext-network-id>
Option Required Description --network yes External network ID to allocate from

Tip

Find your external network with shark network list — look for networks with External = True.

docs.title: associate
docs.text:
Associate a floating IP with a port (typically the port of a server). This makes the server reachable from the internet on that public IP.

shark floating-ip associate <floating-ip-id> --port-id <port-id>
shark floating-ip associate <floating-ip-id> --port-id <port-id> --fixed-ip 10.0.0.5
Option Required Description --port-id yes Port ID to associate with --fixed-ip no Fixed IP on the port (if the port has multiple IPs)

Tip

Find a server's port ID with shark server list-interfaces <server-id>.

docs.title: disassociate
docs.text:
Disassociate a floating IP from its port. The IP remains allocated and can be re-associated later.

shark floating-ip disassociate <floating-ip-id>

docs.title: delete
docs.text:
Release a floating IP back to the external network pool. The IP is no longer reserved. Asks for confirmation.

shark floating-ip delete <floating-ip-id>
shark floating-ip delete <floating-ip-id> -y

docs.title: Full Example: Make a Server Public
docs.text:
# 1. Allocate a floating IP
shark floating-ip create --network <ext-network-id>
# → Floating IP 203.0.113.42 allocated (fip-id)

# 2. Find the server's port
shark server list-interfaces <server-id>

# 3. Associate
shark floating-ip associate <fip-id> --port-id <port-id>

# 4. SSH to server
ssh -i ~/.ssh/shark-my-key ubuntu@203.0.113.42

# 5. Later: disassociate and release
shark floating-ip disassociate <fip-id>
shark floating-ip delete <fip-id> -y

docs.title: Images — shark image
docs.text: Manage Glance images. Images are the base OS templates used to create servers. You can list available images, upload custom images, download them, and manage their metadata and tags.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all images in the project, sorted by name. Shows ID, name, status, min disk/RAM, and size.

shark image list

docs.title: show
docs.text:
Display detailed metadata for an image: format, visibility, OS info, size, and timestamps.

shark image show <image-id>

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Create a new image record in Glance. Optionally upload image data from a local file in the same step.

# Create metadata only
shark image create "Ubuntu 24.04"

# Create and upload in one step
shark image create "Ubuntu 24.04" --file ubuntu-24.04.qcow2

# With custom format
shark image create "Flatcar" --disk-format raw --file flatcar.img
Option Default Description --disk-format qcow2 raw, qcow2, vmdk, vdi, vhd, vhdx, iso, aki, ari, ami --container-format bare bare, ovf, ova, aki, ari, ami, docker --min-disk 0 Minimum disk size in GB --min-ram 0 Minimum RAM in MB --visibility private private, shared, community, public --file — Upload image data from this file

docs.title: upload
docs.text:
Upload image data to an existing image (must be in queued status). Streams from disk without loading into memory — supports large files.

shark image upload <image-id> /path/to/ubuntu.qcow2

docs.title: download
docs.text:
Download image data to a local file. Streams to disk with a progress bar.

shark image download <image-id> -o /tmp/my-image.qcow2
Option Required Description -o / --output yes Output file path

docs.title: update
docs.text:
Update image properties using JSON-Patch. Useful for changing name, visibility, or min disk/RAM requirements.

shark image update <image-id> --name "New Name"
shark image update <image-id> --visibility shared
shark image update <image-id> --min-disk 10 --min-ram 512
Option Description --name New name --min-disk New minimum disk (GB) --min-ram New minimum RAM (MB) --visibility private, shared, community, public

docs.title: delete
docs.text:
Permanently delete an image and its data. Asks for confirmation.

shark image delete <image-id>
shark image delete <image-id> -y

docs.title: deactivate
docs.text:
Deactivate an image — the metadata stays visible but the image data becomes unavailable for download or server creation.

shark image deactivate <image-id>

docs.title: reactivate
docs.text:
Reactivate a deactivated image, making its data available again.

shark image reactivate <image-id>

docs.title: tag-add
docs.text:
Add a tag to an image. Tags are arbitrary strings useful for filtering and organisation.

shark image tag-add <image-id> production

docs.title: tag-delete
docs.text:
Remove a tag from an image.

shark image tag-delete <image-id> production

docs.title: Key Pairs — shark keypair
docs.text: Manage SSH key pairs (Nova). Key pairs are used to inject SSH public keys into servers at boot for password-less authentication. You can generate keys server-side, generate them locally and upload the public half, or import existing keys.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all key pairs in the project with their type and fingerprint.

shark keypair list

docs.title: show
docs.text:
Display key pair details including the public key content.

shark keypair show my-key

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Generate a key pair server-side. The private key is returned once and saved to disk. The public key is stored in Nova.

shark keypair create my-key
shark keypair create my-key --save-to /tmp/my-key.pem
Option Default Description --save-to ~/.ssh/<name>.pem Path to save the private key

Warning

The private key is only returned once. If lost, delete and recreate the key pair.

docs.title: generate
docs.text:
Generate a key pair locally using ssh-keygen and upload the public key to OpenStack. The private key never leaves your machine. This is the recommended method.

shark keypair generate my-key
shark keypair generate my-key --type rsa --bits 4096
shark keypair generate my-key --save-to ~/.ssh/custom-key
Option Default Description --type ed25519 ed25519, rsa, ecdsa --bits 4096 (RSA only) Key size --save-to ~/.ssh/shark-<name> Private key path

docs.title: upload
docs.text:
Import an existing public key into OpenStack. Useful if you already have a key pair and want to use it with Sharktech servers.

shark keypair upload my-key --public-key-file ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
shark keypair upload my-key --public-key "ssh-ed25519 AAAA..."
Option Default Description --public-key-file ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub Path to public key file --public-key — Public key content as string

If neither option is given, the CLI tries ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub then ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub.

docs.title: delete
docs.text:
Delete a key pair from OpenStack. This does not delete local files.

shark keypair delete my-key
shark keypair delete my-key -y

docs.title: Load Balancers — shark loadbalancer
docs.text:
Manage load balancers, listeners, pools, members & health monitors (Octavia). Load balancers distribute incoming traffic across multiple backend servers to improve availability and scalability.

The Octavia resource hierarchy is: Load Balancer → Listener → Pool → Members, with an optional Health Monitor per pool.

Note

Octavia requires the load balancer to be in ACTIVE provisioning status before most mutations. Wait for the status to settle between operations.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all load balancers with their VIP address, provisioning/operating status, and provider.

shark loadbalancer list

docs.title: show
docs.text:
Display detailed properties of a load balancer: VIP address, subnet, port, listeners, pools, status.

shark loadbalancer show <lb-id>

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Create a new load balancer. A VIP (virtual IP) is allocated on the specified subnet and serves as the entry point for traffic.

shark loadbalancer create my-lb --subnet-id <subnet-id>
shark loadbalancer create my-lb --subnet-id <id> --provider amphora
shark loadbalancer create my-lb --subnet-id <id> --description "Production LB"
Option Required Default Description --subnet-id yes — VIP subnet ID --description no "" Description --provider no — Provider (e.g. amphora, ovn)

docs.title: delete
docs.text:
Delete a load balancer. Use --cascade to delete all child resources (listeners, pools, members, health monitors) in one operation.

shark loadbalancer delete <lb-id>
shark loadbalancer delete <lb-id> --cascade -y
Option Description --cascade Delete LB and all child resources -y Skip confirmation

docs.title: Listeners
docs.text: A listener defines a frontend protocol and port on the load balancer.

docs.title: listener-list
docs.text:
List all listeners with their protocol, port, load balancer, and status.

shark loadbalancer listener-list

docs.title: listener-show
docs.text:
Display listener details: protocol, port, default pool, connection limit, status.

shark loadbalancer listener-show <listener-id>

docs.title: listener-create
docs.text:
Create a listener on a load balancer. Each listener listens on a specific protocol and port.

shark loadbalancer listener-create http-listener \
  --lb-id <lb-id> --protocol HTTP --port 80

shark loadbalancer listener-create https-listener \
  --lb-id <lb-id> --protocol HTTPS --port 443
Option Required Description --lb-id yes Load balancer ID --protocol yes HTTP, HTTPS, TCP, UDP, TERMINATED_HTTPS --port yes Listen port number --default-pool-id no Default pool ID

docs.title: listener-delete
docs.text:
Delete a listener.

shark loadbalancer listener-delete <listener-id> -y

docs.title: Pools
docs.text: A pool is a group of backend members that receive traffic from a listener.

docs.title: pool-list
docs.text:
List all pools with their protocol, algorithm, member count, and status.

shark loadbalancer pool-list

docs.title: pool-show
docs.text:
Display pool details: protocol, algorithm, session persistence, health monitor, status.

shark loadbalancer pool-show <pool-id>

docs.title: pool-create
docs.text:
Create a pool and attach it to a listener (or directly to a load balancer).

shark loadbalancer pool-create web-pool \
  --listener-id <listener-id> \
  --protocol HTTP \
  --algorithm ROUND_ROBIN

shark loadbalancer pool-create tcp-pool \
  --lb-id <lb-id> \
  --protocol TCP \
  --algorithm LEAST_CONNECTIONS
Option Required Description --listener-id * Listener ID to attach to --lb-id * Load balancer ID (if no listener) --protocol yes HTTP, HTTPS, PROXY, TCP, UDP --algorithm yes ROUND_ROBIN, LEAST_CONNECTIONS, SOURCE_IP

* Provide either --listener-id or --lb-id.

docs.title: pool-delete
docs.text:
Delete a pool.

shark loadbalancer pool-delete <pool-id> -y

docs.title: Members
docs.text: Members are the backend servers that receive traffic from a pool.

docs.title: member-list
docs.text:
List all members in a pool with their address, port, weight, and operating status.

shark loadbalancer member-list <pool-id>

docs.title: member-add
docs.text:
Add a backend server to a pool. Each member is identified by an IP address and port.

shark loadbalancer member-add <pool-id> \
  --address 10.0.0.10 --port 8080 --name web-1

shark loadbalancer member-add <pool-id> \
  --address 10.0.0.11 --port 8080 \
  --subnet-id <subnet-id> --weight 2
Option Required Default Description --address yes — Member IP address --port yes — Member port --subnet-id no — Member subnet ID --weight no 1 Weight (0-256) for weighted algorithms --name no — Member name

docs.title: member-remove
docs.text:
Remove a member from a pool.

shark loadbalancer member-remove <pool-id> <member-id>
shark loadbalancer member-remove <pool-id> <member-id> -y

docs.title: Health Monitors
docs.text: Health monitors periodically check backend members and remove unhealthy ones from rotation.

docs.title: healthmonitor-list
docs.text:
List all health monitors with their type, delay, timeout, pool, and status.

shark loadbalancer healthmonitor-list

docs.title: healthmonitor-create
docs.text:
Create a health monitor for a pool. The monitor probes each member and marks it as healthy or unhealthy.

# HTTP health check
shark loadbalancer healthmonitor-create http-check \
  --pool-id <pool-id> --type HTTP \
  --delay 10 --timeout 5 --max-retries 3 \
  --url-path /health --expected-codes 200

# TCP health check
shark loadbalancer healthmonitor-create tcp-check \
  --pool-id <pool-id> --type TCP \
  --delay 5 --timeout 3
Option Required Default Description --pool-id yes — Pool ID --type yes — HTTP, HTTPS, PING, TCP, TLS-HELLO, UDP-CONNECT --delay yes — Probe interval (seconds) --timeout yes — Probe timeout (seconds) --max-retries no 3 Max retries before marking unhealthy --url-path no / HTTP URL path to probe (HTTP/HTTPS only) --expected-codes no 200 Expected HTTP status codes (HTTP/HTTPS only)

docs.title: healthmonitor-delete
docs.text:
Delete a health monitor.

shark loadbalancer healthmonitor-delete <hm-id> -y

docs.title: Full Example: HTTP Load Balancer
docs.text:
# 1. Create the load balancer
shark loadbalancer create my-lb --subnet-id <subnet-id>

# 2. Wait for ACTIVE status
shark loadbalancer show <lb-id>

# 3. Create a listener on port 80
shark loadbalancer listener-create http-listener \
  --lb-id <lb-id> --protocol HTTP --port 80

# 4. Create a pool with round-robin
shark loadbalancer pool-create web-pool \
  --listener-id <listener-id> \
  --protocol HTTP --algorithm ROUND_ROBIN

# 5. Add backend servers
shark loadbalancer member-add <pool-id> \
  --address 10.0.0.10 --port 8080 --name web-1
shark loadbalancer member-add <pool-id> \
  --address 10.0.0.11 --port 8080 --name web-2

# 6. Add a health check
shark loadbalancer healthmonitor-create http-check \
  --pool-id <pool-id> --type HTTP \
  --delay 10 --timeout 5 --url-path /health

# 7. Clean up everything at once
shark loadbalancer delete <lb-id> --cascade -y

docs.title: Metrics — shark metric
docs.text: Query metrics, measures and resources (Gnocchi). Gnocchi is a time-series database that stores metering data from your cloud infrastructure — CPU usage, memory, disk I/O, network traffic, etc. Use these commands to explore resources, inspect metrics, and retrieve datapoints.

docs.title: resource-type-list
docs.text:
List all available resource types (e.g. instance, volume, network). Each type defines attributes that resources of that type can have.

shark metric resource-type-list

docs.title: Resources
docs.text: A resource is a cloud object (server, volume, etc.) that has metrics associated with it.

docs.title: resource-list
docs.text:
List resources of a given type. Each resource shows its ID, type, original resource ID, and metric count.

shark metric resource-list
shark metric resource-list --type instance
shark metric resource-list --type instance --limit 10
Option Default Description --type generic Resource type to list --limit — Max number of results

docs.title: resource-show
docs.text:
Display a resource's details and all its associated metrics (metric name → metric ID).

shark metric resource-show <resource-id>
shark metric resource-show <resource-id> --type instance
Option Default Description --type generic Resource type

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all metrics with their name, unit, archive policy, and associated resource.

shark metric list
shark metric list --limit 20
Option Description --limit Max number of results

docs.title: show
docs.text:
Display a metric's details including its archive policy and available granularities. Use this to find the correct granularity for measures queries.

shark metric show <metric-id>

The output includes the archive policy definition — a list of granularity/points/timespan tuples that tell you what resolutions are available.

docs.title: measures
docs.text:
Retrieve time-series datapoints for a metric. Each datapoint contains a timestamp, granularity, and aggregated value.

# Last hour of data
shark metric measures <metric-id> --start -1h

# Specific time range with 5-minute granularity
shark metric measures <metric-id> \
  --start 2026-04-07T00:00:00 \
  --stop 2026-04-08T00:00:00 \
  --granularity 300

# Last 24h, max aggregation
shark metric measures <metric-id> \
  --start -24h --aggregation max

# Limit number of results
shark metric measures <metric-id> --start -1h --limit 50
Option Default Description --start — Start timestamp (ISO 8601 or relative, e.g. -1h, -7d) --stop — Stop timestamp --granularity — Granularity in seconds (must match archive policy) --aggregation mean Aggregation method (mean, max, min, sum, count, etc.) --limit — Max measures to return

Finding the right granularity

Use shark metric show <id> to see the archive policy. Each policy defines granularity/points pairs. For example, ceilometer-low-rate stores 8640 points at 5-minute (300s) granularity. Always use a granularity that matches the policy.

docs.title: archive-policy-list
docs.text:
List all archive policies with their aggregation methods and retention definitions.

shark metric archive-policy-list

Each policy shows its granularity/points pairs — these determine the resolution and retention period of metrics using that policy.

docs.title: status
docs.text:
Show Gnocchi's internal processing status: number of measures waiting to be processed and metrics with pending measures.

shark metric status

Note

This command requires admin privileges and will return 403 Forbidden for regular users.

docs.title: Full Example: Monitor Server CPU
docs.text:
# 1. Find the server resource
shark metric resource-list --type instance --limit 5

# 2. Show its metrics
shark metric resource-show <resource-id> --type instance
# → cpu: <metric-id>

# 3. Check available granularities
shark metric show <metric-id>

# 4. Query CPU usage for the last 6 hours
shark metric measures <metric-id> \
  --start -6h --granularity 300 --aggregation mean

docs.title: Networks — shark network
docs.text: Manage networks, subnets, ports & routers (Neutron). This is the core networking command group — create isolated networks, assign IP ranges with subnets, manage ports, and connect to the outside world with routers.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all networks in the project with their subnets, status, and external/shared flags.

shark network list

docs.title: show
docs.text:
Display detailed properties of a network: admin state, MTU, subnets, availability zones.

shark network show <network-id>

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Create a new virtual network.

shark network create my-network
shark network create my-network --no-admin-state   # create in down state
shark network create my-network --shared            # shared across projects
Option Default Description --admin-state/--no-admin-state True Admin state up --shared False Shared across projects

docs.title: update
docs.text:
Update a network's name or admin state.

shark network update <network-id> --name new-name
shark network update <network-id> --no-admin-state

docs.title: delete
docs.text:
Delete a network. All subnets and ports must be removed first.

shark network delete <network-id> -y

docs.title: subnet-list
docs.text:
List all subnets with their CIDR, gateway, network, and IP version.

shark network subnet-list

docs.title: subnet-show
docs.text:
Display subnet details: CIDR, DHCP, DNS servers, allocation pools.

shark network subnet-show <subnet-id>

docs.title: subnet-create
docs.text:
Create a subnet on an existing network. Defines the IP range, gateway, and DHCP settings.

shark network subnet-create my-subnet \
  --network-id <network-id> \
  --cidr 10.0.0.0/24 \
  --gateway 10.0.0.1

# IPv6 with custom DNS
shark network subnet-create v6-subnet \
  --network-id <id> \
  --cidr fd00::/64 \
  --ip-version 6 \
  --dns 2001:4860:4860::8888
Option Default Description --network-id required Parent network ID --cidr required CIDR (e.g. 10.0.0.0/24) --ip-version 4 4 or 6 --gateway auto Gateway IP --dhcp/--no-dhcp True Enable DHCP --dns — DNS nameserver (repeatable)

docs.title: subnet-delete
docs.text:
Delete a subnet. No ports may be using it.

shark network subnet-delete <subnet-id> -y

docs.title: port-list
docs.text:
List all ports. Optionally filter by network.

shark network port-list
shark network port-list --network-id <network-id>

docs.title: port-show
docs.text:
Display port details: MAC address, fixed IPs, status, device owner, security groups.

shark network port-show <port-id>

docs.title: port-create
docs.text:
Create a port on a network. Optionally assign a fixed IP and name.

shark network port-create --network-id <network-id>
shark network port-create --network-id <id> --name my-port --fixed-ip 10.0.0.50
Option Description --network-id Network ID (required) --name Port name --fixed-ip Fixed IP address

docs.title: port-update
docs.text:
Update a port's name or admin state.

shark network port-update <port-id> --name new-name
shark network port-update <port-id> --no-admin-state
Option Description --name New name --admin-state/--no-admin-state Admin state up/down

docs.title: port-delete
docs.text:
Delete a port.

shark network port-delete <port-id> -y

docs.title: router-list
docs.text:
List all routers with their status and external gateway.

shark network router-list

docs.title: router-show
docs.text:
Display router details: external gateway info, static routes, admin state.

shark network router-show <router-id>

docs.title: router-create
docs.text:
Create a router. Optionally set an external gateway for internet access.

shark network router-create my-router
shark network router-create my-router --external-network <ext-network-id>
Option Description --external-network External network ID for gateway

docs.title: router-update
docs.text:
Update a router's name or external gateway.

shark network router-update <router-id> --name new-name
shark network router-update <router-id> --external-network <ext-net-id>

docs.title: router-delete
docs.text:
Delete a router. All interfaces must be removed first.

shark network router-delete <router-id> -y

docs.title: router-add-interface
docs.text:
Connect a subnet to a router. This enables routing between the subnet and the router's external gateway.

shark network router-add-interface <router-id> --subnet-id <subnet-id>

docs.title: router-remove-interface
docs.text:
Disconnect a subnet from a router.

shark network router-remove-interface <router-id> --subnet-id <subnet-id>

docs.title: Full Example: Private Network with Internet Access
docs.text:
# 1. Create network and subnet
shark network create my-network
shark network subnet-create my-subnet \
  --network-id <network-id> \
  --cidr 10.0.0.0/24 \
  --gateway 10.0.0.1

# 2. Create router with external gateway
shark network router-create my-router \
  --external-network <ext-network-id>

# 3. Attach subnet to router
shark network router-add-interface <router-id> \
  --subnet-id <subnet-id>

# 4. Create a server on this network
shark server create my-vm \
  --name my-vm --flavor <id> --image <id> \
  --network <network-id>

docs.title: Secrets — shark secret
docs.text: Manage secrets and containers (Barbican key-manager). Barbican securely stores sensitive data — passwords, API keys, symmetric/asymmetric keys, and certificates. All payloads are encrypted at rest.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all secrets in the project with their name, type, algorithm, status, and creation date.

shark secret list
shark secret list --limit 20
Option Description --limit Max number of results

docs.title: show
docs.text:
Display secret metadata: name, type, status, algorithm, bit length, content types, expiration, timestamps. The payload itself is not shown — use get-payload for that.

shark secret show <secret-id>

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Create a new secret. You can store the payload inline or create metadata first and upload the payload later.

# Store a password
shark secret create db-password \
  --payload "s3cret!" \
  --secret-type passphrase

# Store a symmetric key (metadata only, no payload)
shark secret create aes-key \
  --secret-type symmetric \
  --algorithm AES \
  --bit-length 256

# Store a certificate with expiration
shark secret create tls-cert \
  --payload "$(cat cert.pem)" \
  --payload-content-type "application/x-pem-file" \
  --secret-type certificate \
  --expiration 2026-12-31T23:59:59
Option Default Description --payload — Secret payload (inline) --payload-content-type text/plain MIME type of payload --secret-type opaque symmetric, public, private, passphrase, certificate, opaque --algorithm — Algorithm (e.g. AES, RSA) --bit-length — Bit length --expiration — Expiration datetime (ISO 8601)

docs.title: get-payload
docs.text:
Retrieve the decrypted secret payload. The raw value is printed to stdout.

shark secret get-payload <secret-id>

Warning

This prints the decrypted secret value. Treat the output as sensitive.

docs.title: delete
docs.text:
Delete a secret. Asks for confirmation.

shark secret delete <secret-id>
shark secret delete <secret-id> -y

docs.title: Containers
docs.text: Containers group related secrets together (e.g. a TLS certificate with its private key and CA chain).

docs.title: container-list
docs.text:
List all secret containers with their type and number of secrets.

shark secret container-list

docs.title: container-show
docs.text:
Display container details and the list of secrets it references.

shark secret container-show <container-id>

docs.title: container-delete
docs.text:
Delete a secret container. The contained secrets are not deleted.

shark secret container-delete <container-id>
shark secret container-delete <container-id> -y

docs.title: Security Groups — shark security-group
docs.text: Manage security groups and firewall rules (Neutron). Security groups act as virtual firewalls for servers — they control which inbound (ingress) and outbound (egress) traffic is allowed based on protocol, port, and source/destination IP.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all security groups in the project with their description and rule count.

shark security-group list

docs.title: show
docs.text:
Display a security group's details and all its rules (direction, protocol, ports, remote IP/group).

shark security-group show <group-id>

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Create a new security group. By default it includes egress-allow-all rules.

shark security-group create web-sg
shark security-group create web-sg --description "Web servers"
Option Description --description Description for the security group

docs.title: update
docs.text:
Update the name or description of an existing security group.

shark security-group update <group-id> --name new-name
shark security-group update <group-id> --description "Updated description"

docs.title: delete
docs.text:
Delete a security group. It must not be in use by any port. Asks for confirmation.

shark security-group delete <group-id>
shark security-group delete <group-id> -y

docs.title: rule-add
docs.text:
Add a firewall rule to a security group. Rules specify the direction, protocol, port range, and allowed source/destination.

# Allow SSH from anywhere
shark security-group rule-add <sg-id> \
  --direction ingress --protocol tcp --port-min 22

# Allow HTTP/HTTPS from specific CIDR
shark security-group rule-add <sg-id> \
  --direction ingress --protocol tcp \
  --port-min 80 --port-max 443 \
  --remote-ip 0.0.0.0/0

# Allow ICMP (ping)
shark security-group rule-add <sg-id> \
  --direction ingress --protocol icmp

# Allow traffic from another security group
shark security-group rule-add <sg-id> \
  --direction ingress --protocol tcp \
  --port-min 3306 --remote-group <other-sg-id>
Option Required Default Description --direction yes — ingress or egress --protocol no any tcp, udp, icmp, or protocol number --port-min no any Minimum port (or single port) --port-max no = port-min Maximum port --remote-ip no — Remote IP prefix (CIDR) --remote-group no — Remote security group ID --ethertype no IPv4 IPv4 or IPv6

docs.title: rule-delete
docs.text:
Delete a specific security group rule by its ID. Asks for confirmation.

shark security-group rule-delete <rule-id>
shark security-group rule-delete <rule-id> -y

docs.title: Full Example: Web Server Security Group
docs.text:
# Create the group
shark security-group create web-sg --description "Web servers"

# Allow SSH from office
shark security-group rule-add <sg-id> \
  --direction ingress --protocol tcp --port-min 22 \
  --remote-ip 203.0.113.0/24

# Allow HTTP and HTTPS from everywhere
shark security-group rule-add <sg-id> \
  --direction ingress --protocol tcp --port-min 80 \
  --remote-ip 0.0.0.0/0
shark security-group rule-add <sg-id> \
  --direction ingress --protocol tcp --port-min 443 \
  --remote-ip 0.0.0.0/0

# Allow ICMP (ping)
shark security-group rule-add <sg-id> \
  --direction ingress --protocol icmp

# Use when creating a server
shark server create --name web01 ... --security-group web-sg

docs.title: Compute — shark server
docs.text: Manage compute instances (Nova). This is the main command group for virtual machine lifecycle — creation, power management, resizing, snapshots, console access, and network/volume attachments.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all servers in the current project with their status, networks, and flavor.

shark server list
shark server list --limit 10
Option Default Description --limit 50 Max number of servers to return

docs.title: show
docs.text:
Display detailed information about a specific server: ID, name, status, flavor, image, networks, key name, attached volumes, and timestamps.

shark server show <server-id>

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Create a new server (boot from volume). A Cinder volume is automatically created from the specified image and used as the boot disk.

shark server create \
  --name my-vm \
  --flavor <flavor-id> \
  --image <image-id> \
  --disk-size 30 \
  --network <network-id> \
  --key-name my-keypair \
  --security-group default
Option Required Default Description --name yes — Server name --flavor yes — Flavor ID (shark flavor list) --image yes — Image ID (shark image list) --disk-size no 20 Boot volume size in GB --network no — Network ID (shark network list) --key-name no — SSH key pair name (shark keypair list) --security-group no — Security group name (repeatable)

docs.title: delete
docs.text:
Permanently delete a server. Asks for confirmation unless -y is passed.

shark server delete <server-id>
shark server delete <server-id> -y

docs.title: start
docs.text:
Start a server that is in SHUTOFF status.

shark server start <server-id>

docs.title: stop
docs.text:
Gracefully shut down a running server. The server transitions to SHUTOFF status.

shark server stop <server-id>

docs.title: reboot
docs.text:
Reboot a server. A soft reboot sends an ACPI signal; a hard reboot power-cycles the VM.

shark server reboot <server-id>
shark server reboot <server-id> --hard
Option Description --hard Perform a hard (power-cycle) reboot

docs.title: pause
docs.text:
Freeze a server in memory. The VM state is held in RAM on the hypervisor. Faster than suspend but uses host memory.

shark server pause <server-id>

docs.title: unpause
docs.text:
Resume a paused server from memory.

shark server unpause <server-id>

docs.title: suspend
docs.text:
Suspend a server to disk. The VM state is written to the hypervisor's disk, freeing RAM. Slower than pause but uses no host memory.

shark server suspend <server-id>

docs.title: resume
docs.text:
Resume a suspended server from disk.

shark server resume <server-id>

docs.title: lock
docs.text:
Lock a server to prevent non-admin users from performing destructive actions (delete, stop, reboot, etc.).

shark server lock <server-id>

docs.title: unlock
docs.text:
Unlock a previously locked server, restoring normal operations.

shark server unlock <server-id>

docs.title: rescue
docs.text:
Boot a server into rescue mode using a temporary rescue image. Useful for fixing boot issues, recovering data, or resetting passwords. Returns a temporary admin password.

shark server rescue <server-id>
shark server rescue <server-id> --image <rescue-image-id>
shark server rescue <server-id> --password mypass
Option Description --image Rescue image ID (optional, uses default if omitted) --password Admin password for rescue mode

docs.title: unrescue
docs.text:
Exit rescue mode and reboot the server with its original boot disk.

shark server unrescue <server-id>

docs.title: shelve
docs.text:
Shelve a server: take a snapshot, then shut it down and free hypervisor resources. Useful for long-term idle servers to reduce costs.

shark server shelve <server-id>

docs.title: unshelve
docs.text:
Restore a shelved server — re-provision it from the snapshot and boot it.

shark server unshelve <server-id>

docs.title: resize
docs.text:
Resize a server to a different flavor (change vCPUs, RAM, disk). The server enters VERIFY_RESIZE status and must be confirmed or reverted.

shark server resize <server-id> --flavor <new-flavor-id>
Option Required Description --flavor yes Target flavor ID

docs.title: confirm-resize
docs.text:
Confirm a pending resize. The original resources are freed.

shark server confirm-resize <server-id>

docs.title: revert-resize
docs.text:
Revert a pending resize and restore the original flavor.

shark server revert-resize <server-id>

Resize workflow

resize → VERIFY_RESIZE → confirm-resize  (keep new flavor)
                        → revert-resize   (rollback)

docs.title: rebuild
docs.text:
Reinstall a server with a new image. The server keeps its ID, IPs, and volumes, but the root disk is replaced. Destructive — asks for confirmation.

shark server rebuild <server-id> --image <new-image-id>
shark server rebuild <server-id> --image <id> --name new-name --password newpass -y
Option Required Description --image yes New image ID --name no New server name --password no New admin password -y no Skip confirmation

docs.title: rename
docs.text:
Rename a server without affecting its state or configuration.

shark server rename <server-id> new-name

docs.title: create-image
docs.text:
Create a snapshot image from a running or stopped server. The image appears in shark image list once complete.

shark server create-image <server-id> my-snapshot

docs.title: password
docs.text:
Retrieve and decrypt the server's admin password. The password is encrypted with your SSH public key at boot and decrypted locally with your private key (RSA only).

shark server password <server-id>
shark server password <server-id> --key ~/.ssh/my-key.pem
shark server password <server-id> --raw   # print encrypted base64
Option Description --key Path to RSA private key (auto-detected from ~/.ssh/shark-* if omitted) --raw Print encrypted password without decrypting

docs.title: console-log
docs.text:
Display the server's boot console output (serial log). Useful for debugging boot failures.

shark server console-log <server-id>
shark server console-log <server-id> --lines 100
shark server console-log <server-id> --lines 0   # all output
Option Default Description --lines 50 Number of lines to retrieve (0 = all)

docs.title: console-url
docs.text:
Get a URL to access the server's graphical console (VNC, SPICE, or serial) in your browser.

shark server console-url <server-id>
shark server console-url <server-id> --type spice-html5
shark server console-url <server-id> --type serial
Option Default Choices --type novnc novnc, xvpvnc, spice-html5, rdp-html5, serial

docs.title: attach-volume
docs.text:
Attach a Cinder volume to a server. The volume appears as a block device inside the VM.

shark server attach-volume <server-id> <volume-id>
shark server attach-volume <server-id> <volume-id> --device /dev/vdc
Option Description --device Device name (e.g. /dev/vdb). Auto-assigned if omitted

docs.title: detach-volume
docs.text:
Detach a volume from a server. The volume returns to available status.

shark server detach-volume <server-id> <volume-id>

docs.title: list-volumes
docs.text:
List all volumes currently attached to a server with their device names.

shark server list-volumes <server-id>

docs.title: attach-interface
docs.text:
Attach a network interface (port) to a running server. You can attach an existing port or let Neutron create one on a specified network.

shark server attach-interface <server-id> --port-id <port-id>
shark server attach-interface <server-id> --net-id <network-id>
shark server attach-interface <server-id> --net-id <network-id> --fixed-ip 10.0.0.50
Option Description --port-id Existing port ID to attach --net-id Network ID (creates a new port automatically) --fixed-ip Fixed IP for the new port (requires --net-id)

docs.title: detach-interface
docs.text:
Detach a network interface (port) from a server.

shark server detach-interface <server-id> <port-id>

docs.title: list-interfaces
docs.text:
List all network interfaces attached to a server with port IDs, network IDs, IPs, and MAC addresses.

shark server list-interfaces <server-id>

docs.title: Volumes — shark volume
docs.text: Manage block storage volumes and snapshots (Cinder v3). Volumes are persistent block devices that can be attached to servers. Snapshots provide point-in-time copies of a volume's data.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all volumes with their size, status, type, and server attachments.

shark volume list

docs.title: show
docs.text:
Display detailed properties of a volume: type, bootable flag, encryption, multi-attach, availability zone, and attachment info.

shark volume show <volume-id>

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Create a new block storage volume. You can create an empty volume, clone from a snapshot, clone from another volume, or create from an image.

# Empty volume
shark volume create --name data-vol --size 50

# From snapshot
shark volume create --name restored --size 50 --snapshot-id <snap-id>

# From image (bootable)
shark volume create --name boot-vol --size 20 --image-id <image-id>

# With specific type
shark volume create --name fast-vol --size 100 --type ceph-ssd
Option Required Description --name yes Volume name --size yes Size in GB --type no Volume type --description no Description --snapshot-id no Create from snapshot --source-vol no Clone from existing volume --image-id no Create from image

docs.title: update
docs.text:
Update a volume's name or description. The volume can be in any status.

shark volume update <volume-id> --name new-name
shark volume update <volume-id> --description "Production database"

docs.title: extend
docs.text:
Extend a volume to a larger size. The new size must be greater than the current size. The volume must be in available status (or in-use if the backend supports online resize).

shark volume extend <volume-id> --size 100

docs.title: retype
docs.text:
Change the volume type (e.g. from HDD to SSD). The data may be migrated depending on the migration policy.

shark volume retype <volume-id> --type ceph-ssd
shark volume retype <volume-id> --type ceph-ssd --migration-policy on-demand
Option Default Description --type required New volume type --migration-policy never never or on-demand

docs.title: set-bootable
docs.text:
Set or unset the bootable flag on a volume. A bootable volume can be used to boot a server.

shark volume set-bootable <volume-id> true
shark volume set-bootable <volume-id> false

docs.title: set-readonly
docs.text:
Set or unset the read-only flag on a volume to prevent writes.

shark volume set-readonly <volume-id> true
shark volume set-readonly <volume-id> false

docs.title: delete
docs.text:
Permanently delete a volume. The volume must be in available status (not attached). Asks for confirmation.

shark volume delete <volume-id>
shark volume delete <volume-id> -y

docs.title: snapshot-list
docs.text:
List all volume snapshots with their source volume, size, and status.

shark volume snapshot-list

docs.title: snapshot-show
docs.text:
Display snapshot details: source volume, size, status, description, timestamps.

shark volume snapshot-show <snapshot-id>

docs.title: snapshot-create
docs.text:
Create a point-in-time snapshot of a volume. Use --force to snapshot an in-use volume (crash-consistent).

shark volume snapshot-create <volume-id> --name daily-backup
shark volume snapshot-create <volume-id> --name live-snap --force
shark volume snapshot-create <volume-id> --name snap --description "Before upgrade"
Option Required Description --name yes Snapshot name --description no Description --force no Force snapshot of in-use volume

docs.title: snapshot-delete
docs.text:
Delete a volume snapshot. Asks for confirmation.

shark volume snapshot-delete <snapshot-id>
shark volume snapshot-delete <snapshot-id> -y

docs.title: Full Example: Data Volume Lifecycle
docs.text:
# 1. Create a data volume
shark volume create --name data-vol --size 50

# 2. Attach to a server
shark server attach-volume <server-id> <volume-id>

# 3. Snapshot before maintenance
shark volume snapshot-create <volume-id> --name pre-maintenance

# 4. Extend the volume
shark server detach-volume <server-id> <volume-id>
shark volume extend <volume-id> --size 100
shark server attach-volume <server-id> <volume-id>
